input_limitations: Several figures and DL axiom blocks are rendered as [FIGURE] placeholders or empty lines in the extracted markdown, but the surrounding prose still describes the model's purpose and use clearly.

models.model_label: OntoCrime: Criminal Core Ontology grounded on UFO (covering Crimes of Omission)

evidence_quotes.quote: The goal of this paper is to engineer a concise core ontology that does not over-constrain reality with unneeded axioms, focusing on tackling the challenges surrounding the Criminal and Positive Legal Theory.
AI & LAW focuses on tackling the challenges surrounding the development of legal ontologies to assign meaning to legal provisions.

Secondly, the inaction of an agent when there is a legal obligation to prevent an undesirable result against a legal right protected by the state is an event that until has not heretofore been properly conceptualized given the difficulties and lack of consensus among the underlying philosophical theories.
In this paper, the assumptions and commitments surrounding the criminal law system are made explicit, through the use of ontological models

goals.justification: The paper explicitly frames the ontology as a means to conceptualize, clarify and assign meaning to criminal-law concepts (especially the under-theorized notion of crimes of omission). This matches the Understanding signal pattern ('conceptualize', 'assign meaning', 'make explicit').

evidence_quotes.quote: A core ontology is justified for indexing domain ontology libraries and providing a standard knowledge skeleton upon which to instantiate other conceptual models.
The full ontology (OntoCrime) can be found at https://github.com/cleytonrodrigues/Tese .

goals.justification: The authors frame the core ontology as a reusable knowledge skeleton/reference for indexing domain ontology libraries and publish the full ontology as a persistent repository — both fit the Documenting signal pattern ('knowledge base', 'reference', 'repository') as asynchronous reference for others not part of the original modeling process.

evidence_quotes.quote: Through these ontologies, it is possible to perform lawsuit simulation, such as the identification of a crime and imposition of penalties. At the end of this paper, we detail these possibilities for future work.
The goal is to build a framework of criminal ontologies, that are accessible through web-based autonomous services capable of performing legal simulations that aid in decision-making.

Finally, an ongoing project is the development of a prototype, in which an ordinary user can simulate lawsuits for real or fictitious cases through a user-friendly interface, without needing to deal with the low-level modeling formalisms.

uncertain_goals.reason: Implementation (LEGIS prototype, web-based services) is explicitly framed as future/ongoing work rather than as a use already realized through this model; per the rule against coding hypothetical/future uses, evidence is weak.

evidence_quotes.quote: an ontological representation through which the normative code can be formalized in a machine-readable fashion to support decision making in the legal domain was proposed: OntoCrime.
The results obtained thus far should improve the juridical understanding of layperson and aid in the labor-intensive task of lawsuits performed by professional jurists.

uncertain_goals.reason: Decision-making support is mentioned only briefly in related-work framing and as anticipated benefits of future systems rather than as a demonstrated use of the present model, so the link to Problem-solving rests on weak/indirect evidence.
